text: The surgeon inserts the laparoscopic scope near the umbilicus, revealing adhesions in the abdominal cavity. Instruments are positioned to begin the dissection of these adhesions.
The surgeon uses the Enseal electrocautery device to dissect and remove adhesions from the omentum, a fatty tissue layer covering the bowel, which contains vessels and nerves.
The surgeon uses laparoscopic instruments to take down abdominal adhesions through small five-millimeter incisions near the umbilicus, aiming to alleviate the patient's pain.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions near the umbilicus, ensuring that the tissue being taken down does not contain bowel to avoid complications.
The surgeon stretches the adhesion area repeatedly to ensure there is no bowel present before proceeding with division, demonstrating careful examination to avoid injury.
The surgeon places a cellulose-based material over the surgical site to create a barrier, aiming to prevent the reformation of abdominal adhesions.

The surgeon rolls cellulose material into a cylindrical shape, resembling a cigarette, and inserts it through a small port into the abdominal cavity. The material is then unrolled and placed in areas where adhesions were cut down to prevent scar tissue formation.
